title: Getting Started: Installing Blender and Navigating the Viewport
html:
Why Blender for Game Art

Blender is a free, open-source 3D suite that handles modeling, sculpting, UVs, texturing, rigging, and animation in one package. For game developers that means zero licensing cost and a direct pipeline into Godot, Unity, and Unreal. This part of the masterclass treats Blender as a game-asset factory: every technique we cover serves the goal of shipping clean, optimized meshes into a real-time engine.

Installing Blender

Download the current Long Term Support (LTS) release from blender.org. LTS builds receive bug fixes for two years, which matters when a course or a studio standardizes on one version. Avoid Steam-only installs if you plan to script or use external add-ons; the standalone installer is the most predictable.

install
# Windows: run the .msi installer # macOS: drag Blender.app into /Applications # Linux (Flatpak): flatpak install flathub org.blender.Blender ✓ # Verify version on the splash screen: 4.2 LTS or newer

The Interface at a Glance

When Blender opens you see the 3D Viewport (center), the Outliner (top-right, a tree of every object), the Properties editor (bottom-right, tabs for render, modifiers, materials), and the Timeline (bottom). The strip of buttons across the top is the Topbar with workspace tabs: Layout, Modeling, Sculpting, UV Editing, Shading, Animation. These tabs are pre-built editor arrangements — you will live in Modeling, UV Editing, and Shading the most.

Viewport Navigation

Navigation is muscle memory. A three-button mouse plus numpad is strongly recommended; if you lack a numpad, enable Edit > Preferences > Input > Emulate Numpad.

ActionInput
OrbitMiddle Mouse Button (MMB) drag
PanShift + MMB drag
ZoomScroll wheel or Ctrl + MMB
Frame selectedNumpad . (period)
Front / Side / TopNumpad 1 / 3 / 7
Toggle perspective/orthoNumpad 5

The colored navigation gizmo in the top-right corner of the viewport does the same thing with the mouse: click an axis ball to snap to that view, drag it to orbit. Below it sit icons for zoom, pan, camera view, and toggling perspective. Beginners lean on the gizmo; pros switch to the numpad for speed.

Tip.
For game art, work in orthographic views (Numpad 5 to toggle) when modeling. Perspective distorts proportions; ortho keeps measurements honest, which matters when an asset must match a grid or collision box.

The 3D Cursor and Selection

The little crosshair-and-ring in the scene is the 3D cursor: new objects spawn there and it acts as a pivot point. Reset it to the world origin with Shift + C. Select objects by clicking; A selects all, Alt + A deselects all. The transform gizmos on the left toolbar (or shortcuts G/R/S for grab/rotate/scale) move, rotate, and scale the active selection. Type an axis letter after the shortcut to constrain — G then Z moves only up/down.

Saving Your Project

Blender files use the .blend extension. Save with Ctrl + S; Save As is Ctrl + Shift + S. Enable incremental saves and auto-save in Preferences so a crash never costs you an hour. Set a sensible startup file: arrange your editors, then File > Defaults > Save Startup File so every new project opens the way you like.

Heads up.
Blender does not auto-save into your .blend path by default — it writes recovery files to a temp folder. After a crash use File > Recover > Auto Save. Still, hit Ctrl+S often.
🎮 Build This: First Scene

Goal: get comfortable moving around and persist a project file.

  1. Launch Blender and dismiss the splash screen by clicking in the viewport.
  2. Orbit around the default cube with MMB, pan with Shift+MMB, and zoom with the scroll wheel.
  3. Press Numpad . to frame the cube, then cycle Numpad 1, 3, 7 for front, side, top.
  4. Press Numpad 5 to toggle between perspective and orthographic — notice the difference.
  5. Click the cube, press G then Z, move it up, left-click to confirm; press Alt+A to deselect.
  6. Save with Ctrl+S as my-first-scene.blend in a dedicated project folder.

title: Mesh Fundamentals: Vertices, Edges, Faces, and Edit Mode
html:
The Anatomy of a Mesh

Every game model is built from three primitives: vertices (points in space), edges (lines connecting two vertices), and faces (flat surfaces bounded by edges). A face with three edges is a triangle (tri); four edges make a quad. Engines ultimately render triangles, but you model in quads because they subdivide and deform predictably. Faces with five or more sides are n-gons — convenient while blocking out but a liability near seams and animation, so clean them up before export.

Object Mode vs Edit Mode

Press Tab to toggle between Object Mode (move whole objects) and Edit Mode (reshape the mesh itself). In Edit Mode the top-left of the header shows three selection-mode buttons, bound to keys 1 (vertex), 2 (edge), and 3 (face). Switching between them constantly is normal.

Tip.
Alt + click an edge selects an entire edge loop; Ctrl + click selects the shortest path between two elements. These two shortcuts alone will double your modeling speed.

The Core Editing Operators

Four operators do most of the work in hard-surface game modeling:

OperatorKeyWhat it does
ExtrudeEPulls new geometry out of selected faces/edges
InsetICreates a smaller face inside the selected face
Loop CutCtrl + RAdds an edge loop around the mesh
BevelCtrl + BRounds or chamfers an edge into multiple edges

After invoking any of these, you can type a number to set an exact value, scroll to add segments (for bevel/loop cut), and left-click to confirm. The operator panel in the bottom-left lets you tweak the result afterward — always check it.

edit-mode flow
# Start from default cube, enter Edit Mode Tab → Edit Mode 3 → Face select # Inset the top face to make a rim I type 0.1 Enter # Push that inset face down to recess it E Z -0.15 Enter ✓ recessed panel

Keeping Topology Clean

Good topology means evenly sized quads, edge loops that follow the form, and no stray vertices. Bad topology causes shading artifacts and breaks normal-map baking later. Useful cleanup tools: M to merge selected vertices (Merge by Distance removes doubles), X to delete (choose vertices/edges/faces/dissolve), and F to fill a face between selected vertices or edges. Use Mesh > Normals > Recalculate Outside (Shift+N) so all faces point outward — flipped normals render black or invisible in engines.

Rule.
Model in quads, triangulate only at export. Avoid n-gons and overlapping faces. Every poly you add must justify its cost — geometry is not free in a real-time scene.

Applying Transforms

When you scale or rotate in Object Mode, the object stores a transform that engines may interpret oddly. Before export — and before adding many modifiers — use Ctrl + A > All Transforms to bake position, rotation, and scale back to clean values (scale 1,1,1). This is one of the most common fixes for assets that import at the wrong size or orientation.

🎮 Build This: Low-Poly Wooden Crate

Goal: turn the default cube into a believable crate using only extrude and inset, keeping clean quad topology.

  1. Select the cube, Tab into Edit Mode, press 3 for Face select.
  2. Select all faces (A), Inset with I and type 0.05 to create a border frame on every side.
  3. Select the inner faces and Extrude inward (E then type a small negative value) to recess the panels.
  4. Select the top face, Inset slightly, and Extrude up by 0.05 to suggest a lid lip.
  5. Add a small Bevel (Ctrl+B) to the outer edges, 1-2 segments, so corners catch light.
  6. Press Shift+N to recalculate normals, Tab back to Object Mode, and Ctrl+A > All Transforms.
  7. Save as crate.blend. Check the triangle count in the top-right statistics overlay.

title: Low-Poly Modeling and Why Poly Budgets Matter
html:
What Low-Poly Really Means

Low-poly is not a visual style alone — it is a performance discipline. Every triangle a GPU draws costs vertex processing, and every object costs a draw call. A modern game might render millions of triangles per frame, but that budget is shared across hundreds of objects, characters, and effects. As a game artist your job is to spend polygons where they read in the silhouette and save them everywhere else.

Thinking in a Triangle Budget

A poly budget is a target triangle count assigned to an asset based on its importance and screen size. Rough modern guidelines:

Asset typeTypical budget (tris)
Small prop (crate, lantern)200 - 1,500
Hero prop / weapon3,000 - 10,000
Background character (mobile)1,500 - 5,000
Hero character (PC/console)30,000 - 90,000

These are starting points, not laws — a stylized mobile game and a AAA shooter live in different worlds. The skill is matching detail to how big the object appears on screen. Detail that needs only a few pixels should come from a normal map (next lessons), never from geometry.

Tip.
Enable Statistics in the viewport overlays dropdown to see live vertex/face/tri counts. Watch the Triangles number, not faces — a single n-gon hides many tris.

Modifiers: Non-Destructive Modeling

Modifiers are operations layered on top of your base mesh, recalculated live and editable in any order. They live in the wrench icon of the Properties editor. Three are essential for game props.

Mirror

Most props and characters are symmetrical. Model one half, add a Mirror modifier, and the other half is generated for free. Enable Clipping so center vertices weld at the seam. You only edit half the geometry, halving your work and guaranteeing symmetry.

Subdivision Surface

Adds smoothing by subdividing faces (shortcut Ctrl+1 through Ctrl+3 sets the level). Mostly used to generate a smooth high-poly source for baking — you rarely ship subdivided geometry in a real-time asset.

Bevel

A modifier version of edge bevel driven by Weight or Angle, useful for adding uniform chamfers to all sharp edges so they catch light.

modifier stack
# Order matters - top to bottom Mirror (X axis, clipping on) Bevel (0.02m, 2 segments) # Apply before export (or let glTF apply on export) Ctrl+A on modifier → bakes to mesh ✓
Rule.
Modifiers are non-destructive in Blender but most must be applied before or during export — an engine sees only the final mesh. The glTF exporter applies modifiers automatically; FBX has an Apply Modifiers checkbox. Confirm it.

Reading the Silhouette

Squint at your model or view it as a flat black shape against the background. If it still reads as a barrel or a lantern in pure silhouette, your geometry budget is well spent. Anything that does not change the outline — surface bolts, wood grain, scratches — is a texture job, not a geometry job.

🎮 Build This: Low-Poly Lantern

Goal: model a readable stylized lantern under 1,000 triangles using the Mirror modifier.

  1. Start with a cube; delete half along X (B to box-select, X > Vertices).
  2. Add a Mirror modifier on the X axis with Clipping enabled.
  3. Block out the lantern body: a tapered base, a glass cage middle, and a top cap, using Extrude (E) and Loop Cuts (Ctrl+R).
  4. Inset the cage faces (I) and extrude them inward to suggest panes and frame bars.
  5. Add a small loop or handle on top using extruded edges.
  6. Add a Bevel modifier (small width, 2 segments) for light-catching edges.
  7. Keep checking the Triangles stat — stay under 1,000. Apply modifiers, Ctrl+A > All Transforms, and save as lantern.blend.

title: UV Unwrapping: Flattening Meshes for Texturing
html:
What UVs Are

A texture is a flat 2D image, but your model is 3D. UV unwrapping is the process of telling the renderer how to wrap that flat image onto the surface — like peeling an orange and flattening the skin. The 2D coordinate space is called UV space (U is horizontal, V vertical), running 0 to 1 in both directions. Each vertex gets a UV coordinate that maps it to a spot on the texture.

Without good UVs, textures stretch, smear, or repeat wrong. Every textured game asset needs a UV layout, so this step sits between modeling and texturing in the pipeline.

Seams: Where the Skin Is Cut

To flatten a 3D shape you must cut it. Those cuts are seams. Place seams where they will be hidden (inside a barrel rim, along a back edge, where parts join) because the texture can break slightly across a seam. Select edges in Edit Mode and use Edge > Mark Seam (or right-click menu). Marked seams show as red lines.

Tip.
Think like a tailor. Cut seams where clothing would have them — armpits, insides, backs. Fewer, well-placed seams mean fewer visible texture breaks but more potential stretching; it is always a trade-off.

Unwrapping

Open the UV Editing workspace tab. With the mesh in Edit Mode and all faces selected (A), press U to open the Unwrap menu. Options:

MethodUse case
UnwrapGeneral — respects your seams
Smart UV ProjectFast auto-unwrap by angle; good for hard-surface props
Cube / Cylinder / Sphere ProjectionPrimitive-shaped objects

After unwrapping, the flattened islands appear in the UV Editor on the left. The mesh-to-UV link is live: select a face in the viewport and it highlights in the UV editor.

Checking for Stretching and Overlap

Apply a checker texture to verify quality. In the UV editor header choose the test grid, or assign a checker material. Squares should stay roughly square and the same size across the whole model. Stretched rectangles mean stretched textures; uneven square sizes mean inconsistent texel density (texture resolution per surface area).

unwrap flow
# Edit Mode, edge select 2 edge mode # Select hidden edges, mark them Edge > Mark Seam ✓ red lines appear A select all faces U > Unwrap # Inspect with checker - squares should stay square ✗ stretched = add a seam

Packing the Islands

For real-time assets you want a single square texture (1024, 2048, 4096 px). All UV islands must fit inside the 0-1 square without overlapping (unless intentionally sharing texture, e.g. mirrored parts). Select all in the UV editor (A) and use UV > Pack Islands. Set a small margin (0.005-0.01) so neighboring islands do not bleed into each other when the texture is mipmapped. Scale islands so important, close-up surfaces get more texture space — that is deliberate texel density allocation.

Rule.
Keep UVs inside the 0-1 space for game assets unless you are intentionally tiling. Use one consistent texel density per object so nothing looks blurrier than its neighbors.
🎮 Build This: Unwrap the Lantern

Goal: produce a clean, packed, non-overlapping UV layout for your low-poly prop.

  1. Open lantern.blend, switch to the UV Editing workspace, Tab into Edit Mode.
  2. In edge mode (2), mark seams on hidden edges — the back of the body, inside the cage, under the cap.
  3. Select all faces (A) and press U > Unwrap.
  4. Apply a checker texture and orbit the model; add seams anywhere squares stretch.
  5. In the UV editor select all, run UV > Pack Islands with a 0.01 margin.
  6. Scale the cage/glass island slightly larger if it is seen up close; keep everything inside the 0-1 square.
  7. Save. You now have UVs ready for texturing and baking.

title: Materials and Texturing: PBR Workflow and Normal Map Baking
html:
What PBR Is

Physically Based Rendering (PBR) is the standard lighting model in modern engines. Instead of hand-faking shading, you describe a surface with a small set of texture maps and the engine computes realistic light response that holds up under any lighting. The metal/roughness workflow (used by glTF, Unity URP, Unreal, Godot) relies on four core maps:

MapControlsType
Albedo (Base Color)The raw surface color, no lighting baked inColor (sRGB)
NormalFake surface bumps via per-pixel normalsNon-color (linear)
RoughnessMatte (white) to glossy (black)Non-color, grayscale
MetallicNon-metal (black) to metal (white)Non-color, grayscale
Rule.
Albedo is set to sRGB color space; normal, roughness, and metallic are Non-Color data. Getting this wrong is the most common reason a material looks washed out or wrong in-engine.

Building a Material in the Shader Editor

Open the Shading workspace. New objects get a Principled BSDF shader — a single node with inputs for Base Color, Metallic, Roughness, Normal, and more. This node matches the engine PBR model almost one-to-one, which is why it exports cleanly. Plug Image Texture nodes into each input. For the normal input you must route through a Normal Map node so Blender interprets the colors as directions.

node graph
ImageTex(albedo,sRGB) ──► Base Color ImageTex(metallic,NonColor) ──► Metallic ImageTex(roughness,NonColor) ──► Roughness ImageTex(normal,NonColor) ─► NormalMap ─► Normal ✓ Principled BSDF → Material Output

Texturing Approaches

You can paint maps in Blender's Texture Paint mode, generate them procedurally with nodes, or use an external tool like the free Materialize or a Substance-style app and import the maps. Many studios use procedural smart-materials; for this course we will hand-assign tiling PBR textures and then add unique surface detail through baking.

Why Bake Normal Maps

A normal map lets a flat, low-poly face look like it has bumps, grooves, rivets, and grain — without any extra geometry. The trick: build a high-poly version with all that detail (sculpted or modeled), then bake the difference between high and low onto the low-poly's normal map. The engine renders cheap geometry but expensive-looking surfaces. This is the single biggest technique behind modern game-art efficiency.

The Baking Process

Baking in Blender uses the Cycles render engine. The workflow:

  1. Have a low-poly mesh (UV-unwrapped) and a high-poly mesh occupying the same space.
  2. Create a new image (e.g. 2048x2048) and add it as an Image Texture node on the low-poly material, with the node selected but not connected — Cycles bakes into the active image node.
  3. Select the high-poly first, then shift-select the low-poly so it is active.
  4. In Render Properties set engine to Cycles. In the Bake panel choose Normal, enable Selected to Active, and set a small Extrusion/Ray Distance (a cage) so rays catch detail without skipping.
  5. Click Bake. Save the resulting image as a PNG and plug it into the Normal input (through a Normal Map node).
Tip.
If the bake shows red/blue artifacts or missing edges, increase Ray Distance or use a cage object. Always bake normals in tangent space for game assets — it deforms correctly with animation.
🎮 Build This: Texture and Bake the Lantern

Goal: give your unwrapped prop a full PBR material and bake surface detail from a high-poly version.

  1. In the Shading workspace, add a Principled BSDF if not present, and plug in albedo (sRGB), roughness, and metallic (Non-Color) textures for metal and glass.
  2. Duplicate the lantern, add a Subdivision Surface modifier, and add detail (bevels, dents) to make a high-poly source.
  3. Create a new 2048x2048 image, add it as a selected Image Texture node on the low-poly material.
  4. Set render engine to Cycles, select high-poly then shift-select low-poly.
  5. In the Bake panel choose Normal, enable Selected to Active, set Ray Distance, and Bake.
  6. Save the baked PNG, route it through a Normal Map node into the Normal input.
  7. Check the result in Material Preview shading — the flat low-poly now shows the high-poly detail. Save.

title: Sculpting Basics: Organic Detail and High-Poly Source Meshes
html:
What Sculpting Is For

Box modeling is great for hard-surface props, but organic shapes — creatures, faces, cloth wrinkles, rock, wood grain, weathered metal — are painful to push vertex by vertex. Sculpting lets you treat the mesh like digital clay, pushing and pulling millions of polygons with brushes. In a game pipeline sculpting almost always produces a high-poly source mesh whose detail you bake down onto a low-poly model (as in the previous lesson). You rarely ship a sculpt directly.

Entering Sculpt Mode

Switch to the Sculpting workspace tab, or pick Sculpt Mode from the mode dropdown. The left toolbar holds the brushes; the top header has brush radius, strength, and symmetry settings. Brush size is F (drag, then click), strength is Shift+F, and holding Ctrl inverts a brush (Draw becomes carve-in, Inflate becomes deflate).

The Core Brushes

BrushUse
DrawAdd or (Ctrl) subtract surface bumps
Clay StripsBuild up volume in flat strokes — workhorse for forms
Grab (G)Pull large areas to block out shapes
Smooth (hold Shift)Relax and blend any stroke
CreaseSharp grooves — cracks, panel lines
InflatePush surface out along normals
Tip.
Hold Shift with any brush to temporarily smooth. Sculpting is a rhythm of build-up then smooth-down. Turn on X symmetry in the header for anything bilateral so both sides sculpt at once.

Getting Enough Geometry: Dyntopo and Multires

Sculpting fine detail needs lots of polygons. Two ways to get them:

  • Dynamic Topology (Dyntopo): dynamically adds geometry under the brush as you sculpt. Great for free-form blocking, but produces uneven triangles, so it is best for the bake source, not for animation.
  • Multiresolution modifier: subdivides the mesh into levels you can sculpt on and step between (sculpt coarse on level 1, fine on level 4). It keeps clean quad topology and can itself be baked.

A third option, Remesh (Ctrl+R in sculpt, or the Remesh panel), rebuilds the mesh into uniform voxels — perfect when a blocked-out shape gets messy and you want even density before detailing.

sculpt-to-bake pipeline
# 1. Block big forms with Grab + Clay Strips # 2. Remesh to even density Remesh voxel 0.02 ✓ # 3. Detail: Crease for cracks, Draw for grain # 4. This high-poly = bake source # 5. Bake normals → low-poly (Lesson 7.5)

From Sculpt to Game Asset

A finished sculpt may have millions of triangles — unusable in-engine. You generate a low-poly version either by retopology (rebuilding clean low-res geometry over the sculpt, manually or with the Quad Remesher-style tools) or by reusing your original box model. Then you UV-unwrap the low-poly and bake the sculpt's normal (and ambient occlusion) detail onto it. The sculpt's job ends at the bake — it lives in your source files, never in the shipped game.

Rule.
Never export a raw sculpt to a game engine. The sculpt is a source; the deliverable is a low-poly mesh carrying baked maps. Keep the sculpt in your working .blend for re-baking later.
🎮 Build This: Sculpt a Bake Source

Goal: sculpt surface detail onto a high-poly asset to use as a normal-map bake source.

  1. Take a copy of a prop (a crate plank, a rock, or a barrel band), enter Sculpt Mode.
  2. Remesh to a uniform density so you have polygons to work with.
  3. Use Clay Strips and Draw to build dents and bulges; hold Shift to smooth between strokes.
  4. Use the Crease brush for wood-grain lines or rock cracks; enable X symmetry where appropriate.
  5. Add fine detail with a small Draw brush, occasionally smoothing to keep it natural.
  6. Keep this high-poly mesh aligned with your low-poly version in the same space.
  7. Bake its Normal (and try Ambient Occlusion) onto the low-poly using the Lesson 7.5 workflow, then save.

title: Rigging and Skeletal Animation: Armatures, Weights, and a Walk Cycle
html:
Why Skeletal Animation

Game characters animate with a skeleton (an armature of bones) bound to the mesh. Moving a bone deforms the surrounding vertices, so one rig drives unlimited animations — walk, run, jump — that the engine blends at runtime. This is far cheaper and more flexible than baking vertex positions per frame. The pipeline is: build armature, bind mesh to it (skinning), refine which vertices each bone controls (weight painting), then author actions on the timeline.

Building an Armature

Add an armature with Shift+A > Armature > Single Bone. Enter Edit Mode on the armature to position bones: select a bone's tip and Extrude (E) to chain a new bone, just like extruding mesh. Build a simple biped: a root/hip bone, a spine, two leg chains (thigh, shin, foot), and two arm chains. Name every bone clearly (thigh.L, shin.L) — names with .L/.R suffixes let Blender mirror poses and the engine identify limbs.

Tip.
Enable X-Axis Mirror in the armature Edit Mode tool settings so building/posing the left side mirrors to the right automatically. Use Armature > Symmetrize to copy one side to the other once names end in .L.

Binding the Mesh: Skinning

Select the mesh first, then Shift-select the armature so the armature is active, and press Ctrl+P > With Automatic Weights. Blender parents the mesh to the armature and estimates how much each bone influences each vertex, storing those values in vertex groups (one per bone). Now posing bones (in Pose Mode) deforms the mesh.

rig setup
# Build skeleton in Edit Mode Shift+A > Armature E extrude bone chains, name them .L/.R # Bind mesh to armature select mesh, Shift+select armature Ctrl+P > With Automatic Weights ✓ Ctrl+Tab → Pose Mode, test deformation

Weight Painting

Automatic weights are a good start but imperfect — a knee bone might pull belly vertices. Switch to Weight Paint mode (with the mesh selected and a bone chosen). Red means full influence (1.0), blue means none (0.0). Paint to fix bad deformation: add weight where a joint should pull, subtract where it should not. Use a low brush strength and the Blur brush to smooth the falloff across joints so bends look natural rather than creased.

Rule.
Each vertex's weights across all bones should sum to 1.0. Use Normalize All to enforce this — stray un-normalized weights cause vertices to fly off or collapse during animation.

Animating a Walk Cycle

Animation uses keyframes: you pose the rig at certain frames and Blender interpolates between them. Open the Animation workspace. Set the scene to a manageable length (e.g. 24 frames for a one-second loop). A basic walk cycle has four key poses:

  1. Contact (frame 1): legs apart, one heel forward, opposite arm forward.
  2. Down/passing (frame 6-7): weight on the planted leg, body lowest.
  3. Contact mirrored (frame 13): the opposite stride.
  4. Passing mirrored (frame 18-19), then back to frame 1 to loop.

Pose bones in Pose Mode and press I to insert a keyframe (choose Location/Rotation or use a keying set). Make frame 24 identical to frame 1 so the loop is seamless. Group these keyframes as an Action in the Dope Sheet's Action Editor and name it Walk — engines import each action as a named animation clip.

Tip.
Use the Graph Editor to smooth motion, and set the loop's interpolation to keep velocity consistent. Stamp a Fake User (the shield icon) on the Action so Blender does not discard it on save.
🎮 Build This: Rig and Walk-Cycle a Character

Goal: rig a simple low-poly character and author a looping walk action ready for an engine.

  1. Model or import a simple humanoid (a blocky biped is fine). Apply transforms (Ctrl+A).
  2. Add an armature; in Edit Mode extrude a hip, spine, two leg chains, and two arm chains. Name bones with .L/.R.
  3. Select mesh, Shift-select armature, Ctrl+P > With Automatic Weights.
  4. Enter Pose Mode, test bends, then Weight Paint to fix any pinching at the joints.
  5. In the Animation workspace, set the end frame to 24. Pose the four walk keys, inserting keyframes with I.
  6. Copy frame 1's pose to frame 24 for a seamless loop; name the Action Walk and give it a Fake User.
  7. Scrub the timeline to confirm the loop reads clearly. Save the file.

title: Export Pipeline and Optimization: glTF/FBX into a Game Engine
html:
Choosing a Format

Two formats dominate game-art handoff. glTF (.glb/.gltf) is an open, modern standard — it carries meshes, PBR materials, textures, skins, and animations in one file and maps almost perfectly onto the Principled BSDF. FBX is older and proprietary but still the default for Unity and Unreal. As a rule: prefer glTF for Godot and for clean PBR transfer; use FBX when a Unity/Unreal pipeline expects it.

EngineBest format
GodotglTF (.glb) — native, recommended
UnityFBX (or glTF via package)
UnrealFBX or glTF

The Scale and Orientation Gotchas

This is where most assets break. Two long-standing conventions collide:

  • Units: Blender's default unit is meters, which matches Godot, Unity, and Unreal — but only if you actually modeled at real-world scale and applied your transforms. A character should be roughly 1.8 units tall, not 0.018 or 180.
  • Up axis: Blender is Z-up; most engines (Unity, glTF) are Y-up. The exporter has Forward and Up settings to convert. The glTF exporter handles Y-up automatically. For FBX into Unity, set Forward -Z and Up Y, or models import lying on their back.
Rule.
Before every export: Apply All Transforms (Ctrl+A) so scale is 1,1,1 and rotation is zero. Un-applied scale is the number-one cause of objects that import tiny, giant, or with broken physics and animation.
export checklist
Ctrl+A > All Transforms ✓ scale 1,1,1 Recalculate Normals (Shift+N) ✓ Apply / enable modifiers on export ✓ UVs present, materials assigned ✓ Armature + Action selected for skinned export File > Export > glTF 2.0 (.glb) Include: Selected Objects, +Y up Data: Mesh, Materials, Animations ✓

Exporting

Use File > Export > glTF 2.0. In the panel: under Include enable Selected Objects (export only what you mean to); under Transform keep +Y Up; under Data enable Mesh, Materials, and (for the character) the Armature and Animation tabs with all actions exported as separate clips. For FBX use File > Export > FBX, set Apply Scalings to FBX All, tick Apply Modifiers, and choose the right Forward/Up.

Optimization for Runtime

Shipping an asset is not the end — it must run fast at scale.

Poly count and LODs

A Level of Detail (LOD) system swaps in simpler meshes as an object moves away from the camera. Author LOD0 (full), LOD1 (~50%), LOD2 (~25%) using the Decimate modifier or manual retopology, and let the engine switch based on screen size. Distant objects costing thousands of tris is pure waste.

Texture atlases

Each material is typically a separate draw call, and draw calls are expensive. Combine many small textures into one larger atlas and re-pack UVs so several objects share a single material — fewer draw calls, faster frames. This is why we packed UVs into one square earlier.

Other wins

  • Power-of-two texture sizes (512, 1024, 2048) for proper mipmapping.
  • Remove hidden faces and interior geometry the camera never sees.
  • Reuse materials and instances; do not duplicate unique meshes that could be instanced.
  • Keep bone counts reasonable for skinned meshes on lower-end hardware.
Tip.
After import, always test in-engine: confirm scale against a known reference, play the animation clip, and check that albedo reads in sRGB while normals/roughness read as linear. A five-minute import sanity check saves hours of confusion.
🎮 Build This: Export and Import Into an Engine

Goal: deliver your rigged, textured character into Godot, Unity, or Unreal with materials and animation intact.

  1. Select the character mesh and its armature. Apply all transforms (Ctrl+A > All Transforms) and confirm scale is 1,1,1.
  2. Recalculate normals (Shift+N) and verify UVs and the PBR material are assigned.
  3. Export as glTF 2.0 (.glb): Selected Objects, +Y up, include Mesh, Materials, Armature, and the Walk animation.
  4. Also export an FBX with Apply Scalings = FBX All and Apply Modifiers on, for comparison.
  5. Import the .glb into your engine. Verify the character stands upright at the correct height.
  6. Check the material maps assigned correctly (albedo sRGB, normal/roughness linear) and play the Walk clip.
  7. Bonus: add a Decimate-based LOD1 and a texture atlas, then re-import and compare draw calls.
